In this example, we will use the parametric binomial method to design a test that will demonstrate hours with a 95% confidence if no failure occur during the test . We will assume a Weibull distribution with a shape parameter .

If the reliability of the system is less than or equal to 80%, the chance of passing this test is 1-CL = 0.1, which is the Type II error. Therefore, the non-parametric binomial equation determines the sample size by controlling for the Type II error.

results.title: A Concise Guide to the Statistical Rule of Three
results.description: Calculate 3 ÷ 100 = 0.03, meaning you can be 95% confident the true adverse event rate is less than 3%. Test 500 products and find zero defects? Then 3 ÷ 500 = 0.006, giving you 95% confidence that the defect rate is less than 0.6%. The rule ...
results.content:
Calculate 3 ÷ 100 = 0.03, meaning you can be 95% confident the true adverse event rate is less than 3%. Test 500 products and find zero defects? Then 3 ÷ 500 = 0.006, giving you 95% confidence that the defect rate is less than 0.6%. The rule ...
The rule of three states that if you observe zero events in a sample of n independent trials, you can be about 95% confident that the true rate is less than 3/n. This relationship comes from the properties of the binomial distribution and gives you a conservative estimate for rare event probabilities.
Calculate 3 ÷ 100 = 0.03, meaning you can be 95% confident the true adverse event rate is less than 3%. Test 500 products and find zero defects? Then 3 ÷ 500 = 0.006, giving you 95% confidence that the defect rate is less than 0.6%. The rule comes from the upper bound of a one-sided 95% confidence interval for a binomial proportion when zero events occur.
The statistical rule of three lets you estimate confidence intervals when you observe zero events in a sample. This technique helps researchers and analysts make informed statements about rare events, failure rates, and quality control scenarios without complex calculations.
Apply the rule of three when you observe exactly zero events in a fixed number of independent trials and need a quick confidence estimate. It works best with sample sizes above 30, where the approximation becomes reliable and performs well for quality control decisions, preliminary safety assessments, or initial trial evaluations.

For this application, the equation can be adapted to apply to a situation in which no failures are expected to occur in the test.

A number of different RDT approaches are available for process engineers to choose from, including cumulative binomial, nonparametric binomial, exponential chi-squared, and nonparametric Bayesian methods.

Now the number of sample sizes can be determined by equating Equation 3 to a significance value (α) type 1 error — in other words, the probability of falsely rejecting a true null hypothesis (Equation 4). Taking logarithms on both sides in that equation and solving for sample size n, you obtain Equation 5, in which the term C is confidence. Thus, to have C% confidence when the CAPA is R% reliable, a minimum of n samples needs to be tested to ensure zero failure as an acceptance criteria.

results.title: Cumulative Binomial for Test Design and Analysis
results.description: The engineer knows the number of test units (N), the number of allowable failures (r) and the desired confidence level (C.L.). With this information, the cumulative binomial equation can be solved for R. Once the reliability value (R) has been determined, it can be substituted into the appropriate reliability equation and the test time can be solved for given the distribution parameters and the reliability value. The cumulative binomial can also be used to determine the reliability that has been demonstrated on a test with few or no failures.
results.content:
The engineer knows the number of test units (N), the number of allowable failures (r) and the desired confidence level (C.L.). With this information, the cumulative binomial equation can be solved for R. Once the reliability value (R) has been determined, it can be substituted into the appropriate reliability equation and the test time can be solved for given the distribution parameters and the reliability value. The cumulative binomial can also be used to determine the reliability that has been demonstrated on a test with few or no failures.
If the engineer has the distribution parameters, he or she can calculate the reliability at the test duration time, otherwise the estimate of the product's reliability can be substituted directly for R. Then, given the desired confidence level (C.L.), the maximum allowable failures (r) and the reliability value (R), the cumulative binomial equation can be solved for the number of units (N). Similarly, if the test is to demonstrate a required MTTF (mean time to failure), at least one of the parameters of the product's life distribution must be known in order to solve for R.
However, if enough failures are encountered on the test, it may be advisable to perform a reliability analysis on the data. In order to determine the demonstrated reliability, you need the number of units on test (N), the number of failures on the test (r) and the desired confidence level (C.L.).
The confidence level demonstrated on a test can be calculated based on the test results and reliability characteristics. Given the number of units on test (N), the number of failures (r) and the calculated or estimated reliability (R), the value of the confidence level (C.L.) can easily be calculated with the cumulative binomial equation.

Non-Parametric Binomial solves for the demonstrated reliability, confidence level or sample size that is associated with a specified test design.
In a zero-failure reliability demonstration test (RDT), the engineer aims to demonstrate a specified target metric (e.g., reliability at a specific time with a given confidence level) by testing a specified number of units for a predetermined time. If no failures occur, then the target metric is demonstrated.
If you wish to estimate whether a failure is likely to occur, one option is to use the Expected Failure Times Plot to estimate the first failure time based on the assumed distribution and parameters. Test Design Method allows you select a test design option. The method you select will determine the required inputs on the RDT sheet. Parametric Binomial determines what test duration or sample size will be required to demonstrate either reliability at a specific time or a specified mean time to failure.
Note that although mathematically it is possible to design a new test with more allowable failures and a longer duration to demonstrate the same target metric, it is unlikely that the metric will be demonstrated by the new test if the original demonstration test failed.) ReliaSoft's RDT tool can assist the user in designing a demonstration test by solving for various values related to the test, such as sample size, required test time, the demonstrated reliability and the confidence level at which the target reliability will be demonstrated.

Note that although mathematically it is possible to design a new test with more allowable failures and a longer duration to demonstrate the same target metric, it is unlikely that the metric will be demonstrated by the new test if the original demonstration test failed.) ReliaSoft's RDT tool can assist the user in designing a demonstration test by solving for various values related to the test, such as sample size, required test time, the demonstrated reliability and the confidence level at which the target reliability will be demonstrated.

The result of this test design was obtained using Weibull++ and is: The result shows that 11 samples are needed. Note that the time value shown in the above figure is chance indicative and not part of the test design (the "Test time per unit" that was input will be the same as the "Demonstrated at time" value for the results). If those 11 samples are run for the required demonstration time and no failures are observed, then a reliability of 80% with a 90% confidence level has been demonstrated.

results.description: Wilson's suggestion of inverting the related score test with null rather than estimated standard error yields coverage probabilities close to nominal confidence levels, even for very small sample sizes. The 95% score interval has similar behavior as the adjusted Wald interval obtained after adding two "successes" and two "failures" to the sample.
